title: Counter Height Farmhouse Table for Four
summary: A 4x4-leg table sized to counter-stool height — a plywood top framed in 1x2, built the same order every farmhouse plywood-top table uses: legs, then apron, then top, then trim.
description:
Counter height changes less about a table's build than people expect — this is the same 4x4-leg, 2x4-apron, plywood-top recipe as a standard dining table, just with taller legs and a smaller footprint sized for four stools instead of six chairs.

The apron sits INSET 1" from the outside face of the legs, which is a small detail that reads as a big one: it's what keeps the legs from looking like they're just screwed to the outside of a box, and instead gives the table a genuine post-and-apron profile. Build both leg-and-apron end assemblies first, then join them with the long aprons and the center stretcher before the top ever goes on.

The top is plywood, glued and nailed straight to the legs and aprons — no separate attachment hardware, no wiggle room for seasonal movement to work against. Trim the edges in 1x2, mitered where visible and cut-to-fit on the ends, which is the standard farmhouse move for hiding plywood edge grain on a tabletop without resorting to a full hardwood breadboard end.

Four stools' worth of standard counter height is 34-36" to the top surface; this design lands the top at 34-1/2" once the top and trim are accounted for, which fits the vast majority of counter-height stools sold as a set.

cutList.note: Cut to fit the assembled top rather than to this exact length.

steps.title: Build the two end leg assemblies
steps.body: Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes on each end of the 25" end aprons and attach to the 4x4 legs, inset 1" from the legs' outside face. Set the apron's height for a 6" clearance underneath — enough to run a foot or a floor mop under, not so much the table reads leggy. Build two identical assemblies.

steps.title: Join the ends with the side aprons and stretcher
steps.body: Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes on each end of the 41" side aprons and attach at the top of the leg sets, keeping the same 1" inset. Attach the center stretcher the same way, or with 3" self-tapping screws if you'd rather not pocket-hole a piece that lands mid-span — center it between the two end assemblies.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Apply glue to the top edge of the leg-and-apron assembly and nail the plywood tabletop panel down with 2" finish nails.

steps.title: Trim the long edges
steps.body: Apply glue to the long edges of the plywood top and the top 3/4" of the 4x4 leg faces. Nail the 48" 1x2 trim pieces on, flush to both the plywood and the leg tops — this is what hides the plywood's edge grain along the table's length.

steps.title: Trim the ends and finish
steps.body:
Measure and cut the remaining 1x2 trim pieces to fit the actual ends of the table — treat the cut list length as an estimate, not a fixed cut. Attach with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue.

Sand the whole table and finish to match your counter stools.

images.alt: Counter height farmhouse table with a plywood top framed in trim, sized for four stools
